content.problemStatement:
Chef constructed a tree with NNN vertices using the following process:


Generate a random array of size N−1N - 1N−1 where 1≤Pi≤i1 \le P_i \le i1≤Pi​≤i. All possible arrays satisfying the constraints are equally likely.
Create a tree with only 111 vertex labelled 111.
For each i=2,3,…,Ni = 2, 3, \ldots, Ni=2,3,…,N, add a vertex labelled iii to the tree, connected with an edge to Pi−1P_{i - 1}Pi−1​.
Find the probability that this tree has a perfect matching. Output the probability modulo 998244353998244353998244353. Formally, it can be proven that the answer can be represented as PQ\frac{P}{Q}QP​ where Qmod  998244353≠0Q \mod 998244353 \ne 0Qmod998244353=0, then print the unique integer RRR in [0,998244353)[0, 998244353)[0,998244353) such that R⋅Q=P(mod  998244353)R \cdot Q = P (\mod 998244353)R⋅Q=P(mod998244353).

A matching is a set of edges such that each vertex is adjacent to at most one edge. A graph is said to have a perfect matching if there is a matching such that each vertex is adjacent to **exactly one edge**.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains a single integer NNN - the number of nodes in the tree.


Output Format
For each test case, output the probability of a perfect matching modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 10410^4104

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1 : There is only one vertex, and that cannot be matched with anyone else.
Test Case 2 : The graph always has an edge (1,2)(1, 2)(1,2), which can be chosen as the perfect matching.
